title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a wildlife conservation project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Guardian of the Wild: Conservation Volunteers

texts.A:
### Sam, The Sea Turtle Patrol
'I spent my nights walking miles of beach,' Sam says. [51]The hardest part was the sleep deprivation; we had to be awake when the turtles were nesting, regardless of the time.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my night vision and general awareness of my surroundings improved dramatically.[/48] [53]I learned that conservation is 99% waiting and 1% action, which requires a specific type of mental discipline.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in data entry helped me maintain the meticulous records needed for the nesting database.[/50] [47]Initially, the relentless humidity and the insects were a shock to my system.[/47] It was a humbling connection to a prehistoric cycle.
texts.B:
### Elena, The Raptor Rehabilitation Center
Elena helped care for injured birds of prey. 'It was a lesson in power and fragility,' she recalls. [47]I hadn't expected the psychological toll of seeing such majestic creatures in cages to affect me so much initially.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the expertise of the senior vets, who taught me how to handle the birds with confidence.[/54] [52]I hadn't anticipated the rush of adrenaline and joy I’d feel when releasing a healed hawk back into the wild.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that humans have a profound responsibility to mitigate the damage we cause to habitats.[/49] [56]If I hadn't seen a documentary about local extinction, I’d never have looked for the center.[/56] It was a powerful journey.
texts.C:
### Marcus, The Reforestation Project
Marcus planted thousands of native trees. 'We were rebuilding a forest from scratch,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the terrain; the steep, muddy slopes made every step a physical challenge.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general fitness and mental clarity reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that planting a tree is an act of faith in a future you might never see.[/53] [50]I found that the organizational skills from my career as a project manager were perfect for coordinating the planting teams.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the silence and the isolation, which I found quite unsettling at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried the task was too big for such a small group.
texts.D:
### Sophie, The Citizen Science Insect Survey
'I wanted to help map the decline of local pollinators,' Sophie says. [49]The project made me realize that even the smallest creatures are linchpins of our entire food system.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the survey if my biology professor hadn't recommended it as a summer project.[/56] [54]I was moved by the passion of the other volunteers, who could identify a bee from twenty feet away.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical strain of crouching for hours to observe flowers to be so constant and draining.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of learning the differences between similar species incredibly frustrating and difficult.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't be 'scientific' enough, but the data was manageable.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological impact of the conservation environment?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions humidity/insects, Elena (B) the toll of seeing caged animals, and Sophie (D) the strain of crouching.

questions.question: notes a positive physical or sensory improvement that resulted from the work?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) improved his night vision, and Marcus (C) saw his fitness/mental clarity reach an all-time high.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on human responsibility or the importance of small creatures?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) reflects on human responsibility to habitats, and Sophie (D) on insects as linchpins of the food system.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the project?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) used data entry skills, and Marcus (C) used project management skills.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to sleep or difficult terrain?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions sleep deprivation, and Marcus (C) the steep/muddy slopes.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having an intense emotional reaction to a success?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) felt adrenaline/joy at the release, and Sophie (D) worried she wasn't 'scientific' enough.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about patience or faith in the future?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) learned conservation is about waiting, and Marcus (C) saw tree planting as an act of faith.

questions.question: highlights the value of the expertise or the passion of others in the project?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) valued the vets' expertise, and Sophie (D) the passion of other volunteers.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the isolation or technical identification?
questions.explanation: Marcus (C) adjusted to the isolation, and Sophie (D) found identifying species frustrating.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or recommendation that led to their involvement?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) saw a documentary, and Sophie (D) was recommended by a professor.
